body:
## Proposal

Explore whether fo should optionally emit findings as [beads](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads) issues — either written directly to a local \`.beads\` store or printed as beads-compatible JSONL on a side channel.

**Shape:** \`fo --beads\` (or \`fo wrap ... --beads\`) writes every finding (or every \`new\`/\`regressed\` finding, once #248 lands) as a bead with:
- title: rule_id + normalized message
- body: file:line + tool source + fix_command (#245)
- severity: mapped from SARIF level
- labels: tool name, rule category
- fingerprint: same stable hash used by #248 — lets fo update existing beads instead of duplicating
- dependencies: optionally link beads from the same file or rule to enable beads_viewer's graph features

## Benefit

### Makes findings actionable across sessions
Today a finding lives for one \`make report\` invocation, then evaporates unless a human copies it somewhere. With beads:
- Findings become durable work items with IDs agents can reference across turns
- \`fixnext\` and similar issue-runner workflows gain a real queue without hand-filing
- beads_viewer's graph views (PageRank, critical path, kanban) suddenly apply to lint/test debt

### Closes the loop with #248 (temporal diff)
- \`new\` finding → create bead
- \`resolved\` finding → close bead
- \`regressed\` finding → reopen bead
- \`persistent\` finding → no-op
fo becomes the bridge between ephemeral tool output and durable issue state.

### Fits existing agent workflows
dk already uses \`issue-runner\` skills and \`fixnext\`. If those skills could consume fo-generated beads directly, the path from \"build broke\" → \"issue filed\" → \"agent working on fix\" becomes zero-friction.

### Strategic
- beads is the emerging graph-aware issue store; aligning fo with it positions both as parts of an agent-native dev toolchain
- No lock-in: beads output is opt-in (\`--beads\` flag); default behavior unchanged

## Open questions

- **Noise control.** Auto-filing every lint finding would flood the bead store. Probably gate on severity ≥ error, or only \`new\`/\`regressed\` from the diff, or require explicit opt-in per wrapper.
- **Ownership.** Does fo write beads directly (requires \`bd\` CLI on PATH and a \`.beads\` dir), or emit JSONL for a separate pipe step (\`fo ... | bd ingest\`)? The latter is cleaner separation; the former is more ergonomic.
- **Fingerprint stability.** Must match #248's fingerprint exactly so diff-events map to bead-events. Share the code.
- **Dependency graph.** beads shines when issues have edges. Does fo have enough signal to emit useful edges (e.g. \"finding A in file F depends on finding B in file F\")? Or is the graph dimension wasted here?
- **Scope creep risk.** fo's remit is rendering. Writing to external stores is a different concern. Might be better as a separate tool (\`fo-to-beads\`) that consumes fo's json output.

## Recommendation

Start with the *consumer-separation* variant: fo emits a stable JSONL schema for findings (already needed for #244-#248), and a separate small tool or \`bd ingest\` step handles the beads write. Keeps fo focused. If that proves useful, consider first-class \`--beads\` integration.
title: Consider: fo auto-creates beads from findings

body:
## Proposal

Give fo memory of the previous run so every report can narrate *what changed*, not just *what exists*.

**Mechanism:**
- Sidecar state file: \`.fo/last-run.json\` (or configurable path)
- Keyed by stable finding fingerprint: \`sha256(rule_id + file + normalized_message)\` — deliberately excludes line number so line drift from unrelated edits doesn't invalidate identity
- On each run: load previous state, classify every current finding as \`new | persistent | resolved\`, detect flakes (resolved-then-new across ≥2 runs), detect regressions (severity increased since last seen)
- Emit a \`diff\` block in the envelope alongside the existing findings list

**Headline band (human + llm):**
\`\`\`
3 new · 2 resolved · 1 regressed · 2 flaky · 41 persistent
\`\`\`

**Envelope (llm/json):**
\`\`\`json
{
  \"diff\": {
    \"new\": [...],
    \"resolved\": [...],
    \"regressed\": [...],
    \"flaky\": [...],
    \"persistent_count\": 41
  }
}
\`\`\`

**Flags:**
- \`--no-state\` to disable (CI runs, first-run scenarios)
- \`--state-file PATH\` to override location
- \`fo state reset\` subcommand to clear

## Benefit

**This is the 10x upgrade for fo's primary consumers (snipe, trixi, any Makefile-driven agent loop).**

### For Claude / agent consumers
- **Context window savings:** agents re-read the same 80% of findings every \`magloop\` iteration. With diff, the agent can read only \`diff.new\` + \`diff.regressed\` (typically <10 findings) and trust that \`persistent\` is unchanged. Easily halves tokens per iteration on a long fix loop.
- **Clear next action:** \"3 new failures since last run\" is unambiguous. \"47 findings\" requires the agent to guess what to focus on.
- **Flake detection is free:** two-run history is enough to flag intermittent failures, letting agents skip them instead of burning turns chasing ghosts.
- **Regression detection:** agents that just edited \`pkg/foo\` can see \"this run added 2 new findings in pkg/foo\" and know their change caused them.

### For human consumers
- **Glanceable headline band** at top of every report: \`3 new · 2 resolved · 1 regressed\`. Tells you in one line whether your last edit helped or hurt.
- **Work-in-progress narrative:** running \`make report\` between edits becomes a conversation with the codebase, not a wall of findings.
- **Dignified resolution:** seeing \"2 resolved\" is the positive feedback loop lint tools usually lack.

### Strategic
- fo's current competitive position is \"renderer for messy build output.\" Temporal awareness promotes it to \"build-health narrator\" — a category where reviewdog, golangci-lint formatters, and trunk.io are not playing.
- Composes with recently filed issues: \`data_hash\` (#244) validates state freshness; \`priority_score\` (#247) can weight \`new\` > \`persistent\`; \`fix_command\` (#245) attaches to every new finding.

## Scope & design notes

- State file is per-repo, per-invocation-target — distinct Makefile targets (\`make report\` vs \`make test-pkg\`) should not clobber each other. Namespace by first-tool-in-stream or explicit \`--state-key\`.
- Fingerprint stability is the core engineering risk. Start with \`rule_id + file + normalized_message\`; iterate if false-positive \"new\" findings appear.
- Two-run history is enough for flake detection (resolved → new). Deeper history adds cost without much benefit.
- First run with no prior state: emit all findings as \`new\` and note \`first_run: true\` in envelope.
- State file format: compact JSON, gzipped if size becomes an issue. Must be diffable for debugging.

## Open questions

- Should \`.fo/\` be gitignored by default (per-dev state) or committed (shared baseline)? Probably gitignored; baseline-as-code is a separate feature (trixi already has \`make baseline\`).
- How to handle file renames? v1: treat as resolved + new. v2: optional Levenshtein-based rename detection.
title: Temporal awareness: diff-against-last-run

body:
## Proposal

When fo multiplexes multiple tools via the `--- tool:X format:Y ---` delimiter protocol, attach an explicit status to each tool slice in the rendered report:

- \`ok\` — tool ran, findings parsed
- \`clean\` — tool ran, zero findings
- \`partial\` — tool ran but output was truncated/malformed
- \`timeout\` — tool exceeded time budget
- \`skipped\` — tool not available on PATH
- \`error\` — tool crashed; include stderr snippet

## Benefit

Today an empty slice is ambiguous: did the tool pass, or did it fail silently? Agents misread \"no findings\" as \"everything's fine\" when the tool actually segfaulted. With explicit status:

- Agents can distinguish \"lint passed\" from \"lint didn't run\"
- CI dashboards can flag degraded runs without parsing stderr
- \`magloop\` can avoid looping forever on a tool that's silently broken

Prior art: beads_viewer per-metric \`status: computed|approx|timeout|skipped\` in its insights envelope.

## Scope

- \`internal/report\` delimiter protocol gains an optional \`status:X\` attribute
- Renderers surface it as a badge/header per tool section
- Wrappers report their own status; report multiplexer reports missing-tool/timeout cases
title: Per-tool status field in multi-tool report stream

body:
## Proposal

For each finding rendered in `--format llm` / `--format json`, include a `fix_command` field with a copy-pasteable shell command that reproduces or fixes the issue:

- Test failure → `go test -run '^TestFoo$' ./pkg/bar -v`
- Lint violation → `golangci-lint run --fix ./pkg/bar/...`
- jscpd duplicate → file paths + line ranges in a grep-ready form
- govulncheck → `go get module@fixed-version`

## Benefit

The single biggest agent UX upgrade. Today the agent reads a finding, reasons about what tool produced it, then constructs the reproduction command — often wrong (wrong package path, wrong flag syntax). With `fix_command` present:

- Zero reasoning needed to act on a finding
- Fewer wasted turns on malformed commands
- Human users also benefit — click-to-copy works

Prior art: beads_viewer `--robot-triage` embeds "ready-to-run shell commands" next to each recommendation; explicitly cited as the pattern that makes it agent-useful.

## Scope

- Extend `pattern.Finding` (or equivalent) with `FixCommand string`
- Wrappers (`wrapdiag`, `wrapjscpd`, `wraparchlint`) populate it during SARIF construction — they already know the tool + location
- Mappers pass it through to renderers
title: Embed ready-to-run fix command per finding
